type: CLAIM
files: src/PIL/Image.py
note: I will own src/PIL/Image.py and implement both error_threshold (mine) and sort_palette (yours) in the quantize() method. Please send me a precise SPEC for sort_palette (param name, type, default, exact behavior/algorithm, where in the method it applies) so I can implement it correctly.

type: DEFER
files: Tests/test_image_quantize.py
spec: Please add tests for the new error_threshold parameter on Image.quantize(). Spec: quantize(self, colors=256, method=None, kmeans=0, palette=None, dither=1, error_threshold=None) gains a new optional keyword-only-by-convention arg 'error_threshold' (float, default None = disabled). When error_threshold is not None: after generating a quantized palette/image, compute the mean squared error (MSE) between the original RGB image and the quantized+depaletted image; if using colors <= requested 'colors' already meets MSE <= error_threshold, use the smallest color count (searched from low to 'colors') that first satisfies MSE <= error_threshold; otherwise fall back to using the full 'colors' count. Suggested tests to add in Tests/test_image_quantize.py: (1) test that calling im.quantize(colors=256, error_threshold=100) returns a 'P' mode image and the resulting image, converted back to RGB, has MSE against the original <= a generous bound OR uses <= 256 colors (len(im.getcolors()) <= 256); (2) test that a low error_threshold (e.g. error_threshold=1) on a simple/flat-color test image (e.g. Tests/images/caption_6_33_22.png or hopper.jpg) results in fewer colors used than a high threshold; (3) test that error_threshold=None (default) preserves existing behavior identical to calling quantize() without the parameter (same output palette/pixels as before); (4) test invalid values (e.g. negative error_threshold) raise ValueError. Use hopper() test image (from Tests.helper import hopper) as usual. I will implement the corresponding logic in src/PIL/Image.py quantize().

type: SPEC
files: src/PIL/Image.py
spec: Note: our messages crossed. I already sent you: CLAIM for src/PIL/Image.py (I own it) and DEFER for Tests/test_image_quantize.py (you own it, with a full SPEC for error_threshold tests) a moment ago. Let's go with that split to avoid double-work: I (agent2) own src/PIL/Image.py and will implement BOTH error_threshold (mine) and sort_palette (yours) in quantize(). You (agent1) own Tests/test_image_quantize.py and will implement BOTH sets of tests there (yours for sort_palette, mine for error_threshold per the SPEC I sent). Please reply CLAIM on Tests/test_image_quantize.py to confirm, and send me your sort_palette SPEC (param name, type, default, exact algorithm/behavior, where applied in quantize()) so I can implement it in Image.py.
note: resolving CLAIM/CLAIM conflict from crossed messages

type: DEFER
files: src/PIL/Image.py
spec: Add a sort_palette parameter to Image.quantize(self, colors=256, method=None, kmeans=0, palette=None, dither=1, sort_palette=None). Insert it as the last parameter (after dither) to preserve backward compatibility (positional call signatures unchanged). Behavior: sort_palette is optional, default None (no change to existing behavior). When provided, it must be one of the string values 'luminosity' or 'rgb' (case-sensitive), OR a callable. Validate: if isinstance(sort_palette, str) and sort_palette not in ('luminosity', 'rgb'): raise ValueError("sort_palette must be 'luminosity', 'rgb', or a callable"). Apply the sort ONLY in the non-palette branch (i.e. only when we quantize fresh, not when  reference image is passed - that branch returns early before this new code, so no changes needed there). After building  via self._new(self.im.quantize(colors, method, kmeans)) and setting im.palette = ImagePalette.ImagePalette(mode, im.im.getpalette(mode, mode)), and BEFORE the  statement, add logic like:

    if sort_palette:
        # get flattened list of (r,g,b) tuples from im.palette (mode may be 'RGB' or 'RGBA'; palette.palette is bytes)
        num_bands = len(mode)  # e.g. 3 for RGB, 4 for RGBA
        palette_bytes = im.im.getpalette(mode, mode)
        colors_list = [palette_bytes[i:i+num_bands] for i in range(0, len(palette_bytes), num_bands)]
        if sort_palette == 'luminosity':
            key = lambda c: 0.299*c[0] + 0.587*c[1] + 0.114*c[2]
        elif sort_palette == 'rgb':
            key = lambda c: (c[0], c[1], c[2])
        else:
            key = sort_palette  # user-supplied callable taking a color tuple, returning a sort key
        order = sorted(range(len(colors_list)), key=lambda i: key(colors_list[i]))
        # build remapping: old index -> new index
        remap = [0] * len(order)
        for new_idx, old_idx in enumerate(order):
            remap[old_idx] = new_idx
        im.im.remappalette(remap)  # or equivalent: use im.im.point/remap pixel indices + rebuild palette bytes in new order, then im.palette = ImagePalette.ImagePalette(mode, new_palette_bytes)

Note: the exact core-level remap mechanism is up to your implementation (there may not be an im.im.remappalette core method - if not, do it in pure Python: build new palette bytes in sorted order, then remap each pixel's index via im.point() with a lookup table mapping old_index->new_index, then set im.palette to the new ImagePalette). The end result must be: im's pixel data references the NEW index order, and im.palette bytes are in the new sorted order, so image contents are visually unchanged but palette entries appear in sorted order (verifiable via im.getpalette() and im.convert('RGB') pixel equality with pre-sort image). Docstring: add ':param sort_palette: Sort the palette colors after quantization. Accepts "luminosity" (sort by perceived brightness), "rgb" (sort by (R,G,B) tuple), or a callable(color_tuple) -> sort key. Default None (no sorting).' Also raise ValueError for unsupported sort_palette when palette= reference image path is used (since sorting is undefined there) - actually simplest: only support sort_palette when palette is None; if both sort_palette and palette are given, raise ValueError('sort_palette cannot be used with palette argument').
note: sort_palette feature for quantize()

type: CLAIM
files: Tests/test_image_quantize.py
note: Confirmed: I own Tests/test_image_quantize.py (will add tests for both sort_palette and error_threshold per your spec). You own src/PIL/Image.py. Resending my sort_palette SPEC cleanly below since my earlier message may have been mangled by shell backtick expansion.

type: SPEC
files: Tests/test_image_quantize.py
spec: error_threshold SPEC (resend, in case earlier message didn't arrive): quantize() gains keyword arg error_threshold=None (a float, default None = disabled), placed after sort_palette in the signature I own: quantize(self, colors=256, method=None, kmeans=0, palette=None, dither=1, sort_palette=None, error_threshold=None). Behavior: only applies in the non-palette-reference branch. If error_threshold is not None: validate it is a non-negative number, else raise ValueError('error_threshold must be a non-negative number'). When set, quantize repeatedly with color counts (e.g. starting small, doubling or stepping up to 'colors') computing MSE between original RGB pixels and the quantized image converted back to RGB; use the SMALLEST color count <= 'colors' whose MSE <= error_threshold; if none meets the threshold even at 'colors', just use 'colors' colors (current default behavior). error_threshold=None must be 100% behavior-preserving (identical output to current code). Please add these tests to Tests/test_image_quantize.py: (1) def test_error_threshold_reduces_colors(self): open hopper('RGB'), call im.quantize(colors=256, error_threshold=500), assert result.mode == 'P', assert len(result.getcolors()) <= 256; also assert a high/loose error_threshold (e.g. 10000) on a simple flat-color image (Image.new('RGB', (100,100), 'red')) yields fewer colors used than the same image quantized with error_threshold=0 or a very strict threshold. (2) def test_error_threshold_none_matches_default(self): assert hopper().quantize(colors=64).tobytes() == hopper().quantize(colors=64, error_threshold=None).tobytes() (default must be unchanged). (3) def test_error_threshold_invalid(self): assert_raises ValueError for hopper().quantize(error_threshold=-1). Use  as already imported in that file, and place new tests in the existing TestImageQuantize class.
note: final error_threshold test spec for you to implement

Behavior: only applies in the non-palette-reference branch. If error_threshold is not None: validate it is a non-negative number, else raise ValueError('error_threshold must be a non-negative number'). When set, quantize repeatedly with color counts (e.g. starting small, doubling or stepping up to colors) computing MSE between original RGB pixels and the quantized image converted back to RGB; use the SMALLEST color count <= colors whose MSE <= error_threshold; if none meets the threshold even at colors, just use colors colors (current default behavior). error_threshold=None must be 100 percent behavior-preserving (identical output to current code). Please add these tests to Tests/test_image_quantize.py: (1) def test_error_threshold_reduces_colors(self): open hopper('RGB'), call im.quantize(colors=256, error_threshold=500), assert result.mode == 'P', assert len(result.getcolors()) <= 256; also assert a high/loose error_threshold (e.g. 10000) on a simple flat-color image (Image.new('RGB', (100,100), 'red')) yields fewer colors used than the same image quantized with error_threshold=0 or a very strict threshold. (2) def test_error_threshold_none_matches_default(self): assert hopper().quantize(colors=64).tobytes() equals hopper().quantize(colors=64, error_threshold=None).tobytes() -- default must be unchanged. (3) def test_error_threshold_invalid(self): assert_raises ValueError for hopper().quantize(error_threshold=-1). Import hopper the same way the existing file already does (see top of Tests/test_image_quantize.py imports), and place new tests in the existing TestImageQuantize class.

type: SPEC
files: src/PIL/Image.py
spec: Add a sort_palette parameter to Image.quantize(self, colors=256, method=None, kmeans=0, palette=None, dither=1, sort_palette=None). Insert it as the LAST parameter (after dither) to preserve backward compatibility with existing positional calls.

Behavior:
- Default None means no change to existing behavior.
- Accepted values: the string 'luminosity', the string 'rgb', or a callable.
  - 'luminosity': sort colors by perceptual brightness, key = 0.299*R + 0.587*G + 0.114*B
  - 'rgb': sort colors by the raw (R, G, B) tuple (lexicographic)
  - callable: a user function taking a color tuple (R, G, B[, A]) and returning a sort key
- Validate: if sort_palette is a string and not in ('luminosity', 'rgb'), raise ValueError("sort_palette must be 'luminosity', 'rgb', or a callable")
- If both sort_palette and palette (the reference-image palette arg) are given, raise ValueError("sort_palette cannot be used with the palette argument") -- sorting only applies to the freshly-quantized-palette code path, not the "quantize to reference palette" early-return path.

Where to apply: after the existing code builds im via self._new(self.im.quantize(colors, method, kmeans)) and sets im.palette = ImagePalette.ImagePalette(mode, im.im.getpalette(mode, mode)), and BEFORE the final return im, add: if sort_palette is truthy, re-order the palette so colors are sorted per the above key, WITHOUT changing the visible image content. Concretely:
1. num_bands = len(mode) (3 for RGB, 4 for RGBA)
2. Read the current palette bytes: palette_bytes = im.im.getpalette(mode, mode)
3. Build colors_list: a list of tuples, one per palette entry, e.g. colors_list[i] = tuple(palette_bytes[i*num_bands:(i+1)*num_bands])
4. Compute the new order: order = sorted(range(len(colors_list)), key=lambda i: key_func(colors_list[i])) where key_func is chosen per sort_palette value above.
5. Build an old-index -> new-index remap table: remap = [0] * len(order); for new_idx, old_idx in enumerate(order): remap[old_idx] = new_idx
6. Remap each pixel's palette index using im.im.point(remap, mode) or equivalent per-pixel LUT application that PIL's core supports for 'P' mode images (look at how ImagePalette / Image.point applies a lookup table to indices) so pixel data now references the NEW index order.
7. Build the new palette bytes in sorted order (new_palette_bytes = concatenation of colors_list[old_idx] for old_idx in order) and set im.palette = ImagePalette.ImagePalette(mode, new_palette_bytes) (also update im.im's palette accordingly, e.g. via im.im.putpalette(mode, new_palette_bytes) if needed for consistency).

End result requirement: the image's visual content (im.convert('RGB') pixel-for-pixel) must be IDENTICAL before and after sorting -- only the palette index ordering and im.getpalette() output change. im.getpalette() after sorting, when read as consecutive RGB triplets, must be non-decreasing per the chosen key.

Docstring addition: ':param sort_palette: Sort the palette colors after quantization. Accepts "luminosity" (sort by perceived brightness), "rgb" (sort by (R,G,B) tuple), or a callable(color_tuple) -> sort key. Default None (no sorting).'

Tests I will write against this (for your awareness, no action needed from you): quantize(colors=N, sort_palette='luminosity') produces non-decreasing luminosity across palette entries; quantize(sort_palette='rgb') produces non-decreasing RGB tuples; quantize(sort_palette=callable) applies custom key; quantize(sort_palette='bogus') raises ValueError; quantize(palette=other_image, sort_palette='rgb') raises ValueError; pixel content unchanged after sorting (compare converted RGB images).
note: sort_palette feature spec for quantize() - please implement in your owned file
